concepts.title: Carrying Capacity
concepts.description: It is the maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely. It can be affected by factors such as food availability, predation, and disease.

concepts.title: Adaptation
concepts.description: Adaptation is a long-term change in the genetic makeup of a population that occurs in response to environmental pressures. It can be an adaptation to environmental conditions or a result of genetic drift.

concepts.title: Species
concepts.description: Species is the highest level of classification in the biological classification system, consisting of organisms that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring.

concepts.title: Population Growth Curve
concepts.description: To remember the stages of the J-shaped population growth curve, use the phrase: 'Jaguar Jumps Joyfully And Jumps Justly'. This represents the stages: J (lag phase), J (log phase), A (acceleration phase), J (j-shaped phase), and J (justly - stability phase).

concepts.title: Confusion between Population and Community
concepts.description: Students often confuse population and community. Population refers to a group of individuals of the same species living in a particular area, whereas a community consists of all the individuals of different species living in a particular area.

concepts.title: Population Parameters
concepts.description: Key population parameters include density, dispersion, population growth rate, natality rate, mortality rate, birth rate, death rate, and carrying capacity.

concepts.title: Species Interactions
concepts.description: Species interactions include competition, predation, symbiosis, commensalism, mutualism, and parasitism.

concepts.title: Genetic Adaptation
concepts.description: Genetic adaptation is a long-term change in the genetic makeup of a population that occurs in response to environmental pressures, resulting in increased fitness and survival of the population.
